coaching.coaching: State the concrete attestation evidence the [operator-attested] acceptance criteria checklist attestation will require (a change id / vote outcome / timestamp), recorded on the ticket.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria checklist attestation

coaching.coaching: Pin down twine check command argument form with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: twine check command argument form

coaching.coaching: Plan how twine dependency declaration in dev toolchain will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: twine dependency declaration in dev toolchain

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: No `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` items found.
findings.finding: The ticket has no `## Acceptance Criteria` checklist. A plan cannot be reviewed for completion without testable, checkable criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` checklist items, one per observable, in-session-verifiable outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix Plan Step 4's `twine check` invocation to match the documented runbook form: `python -m build && python -m twine check dist/*` (add `python -m` prefix and `dist/*` glob so the command actually inspects the built artifacts).
evidence: Plan Step 4: `python -m build && twine check`
docs/releasing.md line 259: `python -m build && python -m twine check dist/*`
.github/workflows/release.yml line 174: `python -m twine check dist/*.whl dist/*.tar.gz`
ABSENCE: the `dist/*` glob that targets the built artifacts is missing from the plan's invocation
findings.finding: Plan Step 4 writes `twine check` (bare) without the `dist/*` glob, diverging from `docs/releasing.md` §1's documented form `python -m twine check dist/*`. Bare `twine check` with no file arguments exits 0 without inspecting any built artifact, making the local validation gate a silent no-op.
findings.location: Plan section 4 (Local gates): `make lint && make typecheck && make test; python -m build && twine check`
scenarios: Executor runs `twine check` with no arguments, receives no error, proceeds to push — but the wheel/sdist metadata defects that `twine check` would have caught are only discovered after the immutable PyPI publish.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `twine check` with `python -m twine check dist/*` in Plan Step 4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `twine` to pyproject.toml's `[dev]` extra (alongside `build`) so `pip install -e '.[dev]'` makes Step 4 work, or update Step 4's instructions to note that `twine` must be installed from `.github/release-requirements.txt` before running the local check.
evidence: Plan Step 4: 'python -m build && twine check'
pyproject.toml dev extras contain 'build>=1.2' but no 'twine' entry
.github/release-requirements.in line 7: 'twine  # distribution metadata validation (twine check dist/*)'
.github/release-requirements.txt line 232: 'twine==6.2.0' (CI-only lock, not a dev dep)
ABSENCE: `grep -q twine pyproject.toml` returns no match
findings.finding: `twine` is not a declared dev dependency in pyproject.toml, yet Plan Step 4 asserts 'python -m build && twine check' as a local gate the author should run before pushing. The `twine` package lives only in `.github/release-requirements.txt` (the CI-only hash-locked toolchain lock), so a developer with a standard `pip install -e '.[dev]'` environment will not have `twine` available and this local gate will silently fail with 'module not found'.
scenarios: Author runs Step 4 in a fresh worktree with only dev deps installed; `python -m twine check` fails with ModuleNotFoundError or command-not-found, giving false confidence that the gate was skipped or broken.
Agent follows the plan literally and reports an error on the `twine check` step, blocking the release unnecessarily.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"twine"` (or `"twine>=6"`) to the `dev` extra in pyproject.toml alongside `"build>=1.2"`, matching what `.github/release-requirements.in` already pins for CI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC in step 7: e.g. `curl -s https://pypi.org/pypi/nava-rebar/json | jq -e '.info.version == "0.10.1"'` for PyPI; `gh release view v0.10.1 --json tagName,targetCommitish` for the tag/release; `git log origin/main -1 --oneline` (compared to released SHA) for the Gerrit merge; `grep -E '^## \[0\.10\.1\]' CHANGELOG.md` for the CHANGELOG section.
evidence: 'Verify PyPI serves 0.10.1; GitHub Release + v0.10.1 tag created; MCP registry + Homebrew autobump follow.' — no command given
AC: '0.10.1 published to PyPI (nava-rebar) with version-lockstep across pyproject/server.json' — no curl/pip proving command
AC: 'v0.10.1 tag + GitHub Release created at the released main SHA' — no gh release view command
AC: 'Release commit landed on main through Gerrit with both gate votes green' — no git log / Gerrit API check
AC: 'CHANGELOG.md carries a curated [0.10.1] section' — no grep command
findings.finding: Step 7 says 'Verify PyPI serves 0.10.1; GitHub Release + v0.10.1 tag created' but provides no concrete proving command for any of the four acceptance criteria — no pip/curl command for PyPI, no gh CLI call for the tag/release, no git command to confirm the commit on main, and no grep anchor for the CHANGELOG section. Each AC is completion-relevant and requires a command that produces verifiable evidence.
findings.location: Plan step 7 / Acceptance criteria (all four bullets)
scenarios: Executor completes workflow dispatch and assumes success without independently confirming PyPI propagation or the tag SHA
CHANGELOG section check is skipped because no verification step requires it
findings.suggested_fix:
Append to step 7:
```
# Prove AC1 – PyPI
curl -s https://pypi.org/pypi/nava-rebar/json | jq -e '.info.version == "0.10.1"'
# Prove AC2 – tag + release
gh release view v0.10.1 --json tagName,targetCommitish
# Prove AC3 – commit on main (compare to workflow run SHA)
git fetch origin main && git log origin/main -1 --format='%H %s'
# Prove AC4 – CHANGELOG
grep -E '^## \[0\.10\.1\]' CHANGELOG.md
# Prove version lockstep
grep '0\.10\.1' pyproject.toml server.json
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the executor's responsibility for MCP registry and Homebrew autobump in step 7: either 'these are fully automated — no action required' or 'verify within N hours that a Homebrew autobump PR was opened at <repo>'.
evidence: 'MCP registry + Homebrew autobump follow.' — no statement of executor responsibility or expected timeline/verification
findings.finding: Step 7 mentions 'MCP registry + Homebrew autobump follow' without stating whether the executor is responsible for verifying or triggering these, or whether they are fully automated post-release. This is a defaultable gap that could cause the executor to either skip verification of a required downstream step or waste time on a fully-automated one.
scenarios: Executor marks the ticket done without checking whether Homebrew autobump PR was opened, if that is actually expected
Executor spends time manually filing a Homebrew PR that is already automated

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out section (e.g., `pip index --yank` procedure or 'cut 0.10.2 hotfix' instructions) covering the irreversible PyPI publish step in Plan Step 6/7.
evidence: Step 6: 'approve the pypi deployment when prompted' — manual gate exists but no back-out instructions follow
Step 7 only describes verification, not remediation
ABSENCE: no mention of 'yank', '0.10.2 hotfix', or any rollback procedure for a bad publish
findings.finding: Publishing to PyPI is an irreversible step (yanking is possible but re-uploading the same version is not), yet the plan includes no rollback or back-out path if the published 0.10.1 artifact is defective post-publish.
findings.location: Plan Step 6 ('approve the pypi deployment when prompted') and Step 7 ('Verify PyPI serves 0.10.1')
scenarios: A defective wheel is approved and published; PyPI consumers immediately pull it; the plan provides no documented path (yank + hotfix release) to recover.
The release.yml workflow publishes successfully but twine check was insufficient and the package is broken at install time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' sub-step after Step 7: 'If the published release is defective, run `gh api ... /yanked true` (PyPI yank) immediately, then cut a 0.10.2 patch; document this in docs/releasing.md as the standard back-out procedure.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add ACs covering MCP registry and Homebrew autobump verification, or explicitly mark them out-of-scope in the plan body so Step 7's language doesn't imply they are required deliverables.
evidence: Step 7: 'MCP registry + Homebrew autobump follow' — stated as a verification item
ABSENCE: no AC reads 'MCP registry updated' or 'Homebrew formula bumped to 0.10.1'
findings.finding: Plan Step 7 describes 'MCP registry + Homebrew autobump follow' as expected outcomes of the release, but no acceptance criterion covers verification of these downstream effects, leaving them as undeclared orphan deliverables.
findings.location: Plan Step 7 ('MCP registry + Homebrew autobump follow') vs. Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: The autobump silently fails; no AC catches it; the release is declared complete while MCP/Homebrew remain at 0.10.0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a partial-failure recovery note to Step 6/7 specifying how to handle 'tag created but PyPI publish failed' (e.g., delete the tag with `git push origin :refs/tags/v0.10.1`, re-run workflow, or manually `twine upload`).
evidence: No mention of rollback, tag-deletion, or workflow re-run procedure in any step.
Step 7 only describes the happy-path verification ('Verify PyPI serves 0.10.1').
Absence of any partial-failure remediation guidance.
findings.finding: The plan has no recovery path if release.yml fails after the v0.10.1 tag is created but before PyPI publication succeeds (partial-state: tag exists, package absent from PyPI). Re-running the workflow would fail on duplicate-tag creation, and PyPI would reject a re-upload of an already-published version, leaving the release in a stuck state with no documented remediation.
findings.location: Plan Step 6 — 'dispatch release.yml … approve the pypi deployment when prompted'
scenarios: release.yml network timeout after tag push but before twine upload completes
PyPI deployment approval window expires; workflow aborts; tag already pushed to GitHub
findings.suggested_fix: Append to Step 6: 'If the workflow fails after tag creation but before PyPI publish: (a) delete the remote tag via `git push origin :refs/tags/v0.10.1`; (b) re-run `gh workflow run release.yml` with the same inputs. If PyPI upload fails after a successful tag+release, use `twine upload dist/*` manually against the existing GitHub Release artifacts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In Steps 6–7, declare the access posture of the PyPI publish path: specify (a) which GitHub role/team membership is required to trigger `gh workflow run release.yml`, and (b) which named principals (e.g. a restricted GitHub Actions environment reviewer list) must approve the PyPI deployment — so the access class of this boundary-crossing action is explicitly on record.
evidence: Plan Step 6 names the boundary-crossing action ('gh workflow run'; 'approve the pypi deployment when prompted') but provides no declared access class for either action.
No mention of required GitHub role/team membership to trigger the workflow_dispatch event.
No mention of which GitHub environment protects the PyPI deployment approval or which reviewers are listed.
OWASP ASVS V4 (Access Control) — access level of a boundary-crossing sensitive path must be explicitly declared.
findings.finding: The PyPI publish path (Steps 6–7) crosses a public trust boundary but the plan never declares its access posture: it does not state who is authorised to trigger `gh workflow run release.yml`, what identity/role is required to approve the PyPI deployment environment, or whether the dispatch is restricted to specific GitHub principals. An undeclared access posture on a boundary-crossing publish path is itself the gap — if an unauthorised actor can trigger the workflow or approve the environment, a malicious release can be pushed to all downstream consumers.
findings.location: Plan Step 6: 'dispatch release.yml from main with version=0.10.1 (gh workflow run); approve the pypi deployment when prompted'
scenarios: An engineer with only read access inadvertently discovers they can trigger release.yml and approve the environment, cutting an unreviewed release.
A compromised GitHub account belonging to any repo contributor triggers an unauthorized publish to PyPI, impacting all downstream installers of nava-rebar.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to Step 6 such as: 'Only maintainers with the GitHub `maintain` role on the repo may trigger `gh workflow run`; the `pypi-release` GitHub Actions environment is configured with required reviewers [list team/individuals], ensuring the deployment is a two-party action.' Confirm this matches the actual environment configuration in `.github/workflows/release.yml`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
